title: 01 - The Basics
description: Foundational C++ questions covering compiled programs, basic syntax, types, constants, control flow, pointers, user-defined types, modularity, namespaces, and error handling.

questions.text: What happens between C++ source code and a runnable program in a typical compiled build?

answers.text: The compiler translates source files to object code, then the linker combines object code and libraries into an executable.
The interpreter executes each C++ statement directly from the source file.
The preprocessor alone produces the final executable.
The operating system rewrites C++ source code into machine code every time the program starts.

questions.explanation: C++ is normally implemented as a compiled language. Compilation produces object code, and linking resolves cross-file and library references to create the program that runs.

questions.text: Which part of C++ defines built-in types, expressions, statements, and core syntax?
questions.imageAlt: Core language and standard library relationship

answers.text: Only the standard library.
Only the linker.
Only user-defined classes.
The core language.

questions.explanation: The core language supplies syntax and fundamental rules. The standard library builds on those rules with reusable facilities such as streams, containers, algorithms, and strings.

questions.text: Why do C++ programs use declarations before many names can be used?

answers.text: A declaration always executes a statement at run time.
A declaration prevents the name from being used outside main.
A declaration tells the compiler what a name denotes and how that name may be used.
A declaration always allocates dynamic memory.

questions.explanation: The compiler type-checks uses of names. A declaration supplies the required information, such as whether a name is a function, object, type, or namespace member.

questions.text: What is the difference between a declaration and a definition in C++?

answers.text: There is no difference in C++.
A declaration introduces a name; a definition provides the entity itself or its storage/body.
A definition is always written in a header and a declaration is always written in a source file.
A declaration runs at compile time and a definition runs at link time.

questions.explanation: For example, int f(int); declares a function, while int f(int x) { return x; } defines it. Some declarations, such as int x;, are also definitions.

questions.text: Which description best matches an object in C++?
questions.imageAlt: Type, object, value, and variable relationship

answers.text: A region of storage that has a type and can hold a value.
Only an instance of a class with private data.
Only a variable allocated with new.
A name that cannot be modified.

questions.explanation: C++ uses object broadly: an int variable, an array element, and a class instance are all objects because each occupies storage and has a type.

questions.text: What role does a type play in C++?

answers.text: It decides which header file is included.
It is only a comment for human readers.
It exists only for class objects, not for built-in values.
It defines the set of possible values and operations for an object or expression.

questions.explanation: The compiler uses types to reject invalid operations and to choose correct machine-level representations and operations.

questions.text: Why is initialization preferred over assigning to an uninitialized variable later?

answers.text: Initialization is required only for const objects.
Uninitialized variables automatically contain zero in every scope.
Initialization gives the object a known value from the start of its lifetime.
Assignment before initialization is always faster.

questions.explanation: Using a variable before it has a known value is a common bug. Initializing at the point of definition reduces that risk.

questions.text: What does a statement do in C++?

answers.text: It only changes compiler options.
It specifies an action, such as selecting, iterating, returning, or evaluating an expression.
It only introduces a type name.
It only allocates storage for global objects.

questions.explanation: Statements are the executable building blocks of C++ functions. Examples include if statements, for statements, return statements, and expression statements.

questions.text: What is an expression in C++?

answers.text: A combination of operands and operators that computes a value or produces an effect.
A file included by the preprocessor.
A class definition with only public members.
A build command passed to the compiler.

questions.explanation: x + y, f(3), and ++i are expressions. Some expressions compute a value, while others also modify state.

questions.text: What is the main benefit of using standard library facilities such as string, vector, and iostream?

answers.text: They disable compile-time type checking.
They make every program independent of declarations.
They replace the need for functions.
They provide well-tested, reusable abstractions for common tasks.

questions.explanation: The standard library avoids repeatedly hand-coding common mechanisms such as dynamic arrays, text handling, and input/output.

questions.text: What does scope control in a C++ program?

answers.text: Whether a function is compiled or interpreted.
How the linker chooses the operating system.
Where a declared name can be used.
How many bytes an int occupies.

questions.explanation: A name declared inside a block is normally available only within that block. Namespaces, classes, and functions also introduce scopes.

questions.text: Why does C++ rely heavily on compile-time checking?

answers.text: It prevents programmers from defining their own types.
Many type and name errors can be detected before the program runs.
Compile-time checking guarantees that no run-time error can occur.
It makes source code unnecessary after the first execution.

questions.explanation: Compile-time checking catches many mistakes early, but programs still need run-time checks for input, resource availability, and invalid states.

questions.text: In the minimal program below, which function is the hosted C++ entry point?

answers.text: A function named main.
A function named start.
A class named Program.
A namespace named std.

questions.explanation: A hosted C++ implementation starts execution by calling main. The function returns an int status code to the environment.

questions.text: In the program below, what does #include <iostream> make available for the output statement?

answers.text: The definition of main.
All user-defined classes in the program.
The machine code for the final executable.
Standard stream declarations such as std::cout and std::cin.

questions.explanation: <iostream> declares the standard input and output stream objects and related types. Including it lets the compiler check uses such as std::cout.

questions.text: In the program below, what does std::cout represent?

answers.text: The C++ compiler.
A built-in arithmetic operator.
The standard output stream object.
A string literal.

questions.explanation: std::cout is the standard output stream. Values inserted into it are written to the program output destination.

questions.text: In the statement below, what does the << operator do with std::cout?
questions.code: std::cout << "Hello, World!\n";

answers.text: It terminates the program.
It inserts a value into the output stream.
It compares two strings.
It declares a namespace.

questions.explanation: For iostreams, operator << is overloaded as stream insertion. std::cout << "Hi" writes the string to standard output.

questions.text: In the statement below, what kind of value is "Hello, World!\n"?
questions.code: std::cout << "Hello, World!\n";

answers.text: A string literal containing characters and a newline escape sequence.
A variable definition.
A pointer declaration with no initializer.
A namespace alias.

questions.explanation: Text enclosed in double quotes is a string literal. The escape sequence \n represents a newline character.

questions.text: In the program below, why is std:: written before cout?

answers.text: cout is a member of main.
std:: converts output to a string literal.
std:: marks a variable as static.
cout is declared in the standard namespace.

questions.explanation: The qualifier std:: tells the compiler to look for cout inside namespace std, where the standard library names are placed.

questions.text: In main below, what does return 0; usually mean?

answers.text: The compiler should skip linking.
All variables become constants.
The program reports successful completion.
The first line of output is erased.

questions.explanation: main returns an int status code. Zero conventionally indicates success to the calling environment.

questions.text: In the statement below, what is the purpose of the semicolon?
questions.code: std::cout << "Hello";

answers.text: It changes cout into cin.
It terminates the expression statement.
It closes the main function body.
It opens the std namespace.

questions.explanation: Most expression statements in C++ end with a semicolon. Omitting it is a syntax error.

questions.text: Which line writes a newline after the word Done?

answers.text: std::cout << "Done\n";
std::cout >> "Done\n";
std::cin << "Done\n";
return "Done\n";

questions.explanation: The insertion operator sends the string literal to std::cout. The escape sequence \n adds the newline.
questions.explanationCode:
std::cout << "Done\n";
std::cout << "Done" << std::endl;

questions.text: What does a compiler diagnose if the closing brace of main is missing?

answers.text: A successful build with an empty program.
A run-time exception only after output.
A namespace collision.
A syntax error.

questions.explanation: Braces define the function body. A missing brace breaks the grammar, so the compiler must reject the program.

questions.text: Which program fragment is a valid minimal main definition?

answers.text: void start() { return 0; }
namespace main { return 0; }
int main() { return 0; }
main int() { return 0; }

questions.explanation: main is a function. In standard hosted C++, it returns int and can be defined with an empty parameter list.

questions.text: The two output forms below both end the line. What is a common reason to use "\n" instead of std::endl for ordinary line breaks?
questions.code:
std::cout << "Done\n";
std::cout << "Done" << std::endl;

answers.text: "\n" only works in comments.
"\n" writes a newline without forcing a stream flush.
"\n" closes the stream permanently.
std::endl is not part of the standard library.

questions.explanation: std::endl both writes a newline and flushes the stream. Flushing can be useful, but it is often unnecessary for ordinary output.

questions.text: In the declaration below, what does the type bool represent?
questions.code: bool ready = true;

answers.text: A truth value, either true or false.
A single Unicode character in every implementation.
A decimal number with fractional precision.
A pointer to a memory address.

questions.explanation: bool is used for conditions and logical results. Relational operations such as x < y produce bool values.

questions.text: In the declaration below, which built-in type is used for whole-number arithmetic?
questions.code: int inch = 12;

answers.text: double
char*
namespace
int

questions.explanation: int represents integer values and supports arithmetic operations such as addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, and remainder.

questions.text: In the declaration below, what is double typically used for?
questions.code: double d = 2.2;

answers.text: Only Boolean tests.
Only namespace qualification.
Floating-point values with fractional parts.
Only character output.

questions.explanation: double stores floating-point numbers such as 3.14. It is useful for approximate real-number calculations.

questions.text: In the declaration below, what does char represent in ordinary C++ code?
questions.code: char ch = 'x';

answers.text: A named block scope.
A character-sized object, often used for narrow character data.
A dynamically allocated array.
A function returning no value.

questions.explanation: char is the smallest addressable character type. It can hold character values and also participates in integer operations.

questions.text: In the declaration below, what does int count = 0; do?
questions.code: int count = 0;

answers.text: It defines an int variable named count and initializes it to zero.
It declares a function named count returning int.
It creates a namespace named count.
It compares count with zero.

questions.explanation: A variable definition creates an object. The initializer gives the object its initial value.
questions.explanationImageAlt: Variable, object, type, and value relationship

questions.text: In the declarations below, why can list initialization with braces be useful for numeric variables?

answers.text: It always allocates the variable on the free store.
It turns every variable into a reference.
It disables arithmetic on the value.
It rejects many narrowing conversions at compile time.

questions.explanation: For example, int x{3.7}; is ill-formed because the fractional part would be lost. Braced initialization helps catch such mistakes.

questions.text: In the snippet below, what value is stored in n?
questions.code:
int n = 7 / 2;
int r = 7 % 2;

answers.text: A compile-time error because division requires double.

questions.explanation: Integer division discards the fractional part. If a fractional result is needed, use a floating-point operand such as 7.0 / 2.

questions.text: In the snippet below, what does the % operator compute for r?
questions.code:
int n = 7 / 2;
int r = 7 % 2;

answers.text: A logical negation.
The remainder after integer division.
A percentage converted to double.
The address of the left operand.

questions.explanation: For example, 7 % 2 is 1 because 7 divided by 2 leaves remainder 1.

questions.text: In the declarations below, what does auto do?

answers.text: It asks the compiler to deduce the variable type from the initializer.
It delays type checking until run time.
It makes n change type after each assignment.
It declares n as a string.

questions.explanation: auto still gives the variable a fixed compile-time type. In auto n = 42;, the deduced type is int.

questions.text: What is the difference between the two operations below?
questions.code:
int count = 0;
count = 42;

answers.text: Assignment is the only way to create an object.
Initialization can only be used for pointers.
They are always identical operations in every type.
Initialization gives an object its first value; assignment replaces the value of an existing object.

questions.explanation: Constructors can make initialization very different from later assignment, especially for user-defined types.

questions.text: Why should a variable usually be declared close to its first use?

answers.text: Variables near their first use are always global.
It prevents the variable from having a type.
It reduces the scope where the variable can be misused and makes initialization clearer.
The compiler rejects variables declared inside blocks.

questions.explanation: A narrow scope improves readability and reduces accidental dependence on stale values.

questions.text: What can happen when signed integer arithmetic overflows?

answers.text: The program always throws std::overflow_error.
The behavior is undefined by the C++ standard.
The result is guaranteed to wrap modulo two in every implementation.
The value is automatically converted to double.

questions.explanation: Signed overflow is not a portable operation. Code that can overflow should use wider types, checked arithmetic, or redesign the calculation.

questions.text: In the snippet below, what does sizeof(int) produce?
questions.code:
static_assert(sizeof(char) == 1);
auto bytes = sizeof(int);

answers.text: The size in bytes of an object of type T.
The number of values T can represent.
The number of functions declared for T.
The run-time address of T.

questions.explanation: sizeof is evaluated as a compile-time constant for complete object types. The result type is std::size_t.

questions.text: Why can mixing signed and unsigned integers be surprising?

answers.text: Unsigned integers cannot be compared.
Signed integers are always larger than unsigned integers.
The compiler treats every comparison as a string comparison.
Usual arithmetic conversions can convert signed values to unsigned values.

questions.explanation: A negative signed value can become a large unsigned value during conversion. This is a common source of comparison bugs.

questions.text: What does const mean for the initialized variable below?
questions.code: const int dmv = 17;

answers.text: The variable is always allocated dynamically.
The variable can be assigned only inside a loop.
The variable cannot be modified through that name after initialization.
The variable has no type.

questions.explanation: const expresses an intent that the value should not change. The compiler rejects assignments through a const-qualified object.

questions.text: In the code below, why is max2 invalid if that declaration is used?

answers.text: It must be a pointer.
Its value must be computable at compile time.
Its value must be read from standard input.
It must be stored on the free store.

questions.explanation: constexpr is used for values that are usable in constant expressions, such as array bounds and template arguments.
questions.explanationCode:
constexpr int limit = 100;
int table[limit];

questions.text: Which declaration is valid for a compile-time array bound?

answers.text: constexpr int n = 10;
int n = std::cin.get();
double n = 10.5;
int* n = nullptr;

questions.explanation: A built-in array bound must be an integral constant expression. constexpr int n = 10; satisfies that requirement.
questions.explanationCode:
constexpr int size = 64;
int buffer[size];

questions.text: Why is max_retry_count better than repeating the literal 4 throughout the retry loop below?

answers.text: A named constant prevents the program from being linked.
A literal cannot be used in arithmetic.
A named constant always changes at run time.
The name documents the meaning and centralizes the value.

questions.explanation: A name such as max_retry_count is clearer than a bare number and makes later changes less error-prone.

questions.text: What must be true when defining a const object?

answers.text: It must be declared inside namespace std.
It must have type double.
It must be initialized.
It must be initialized only after its first assignment.

questions.explanation: A const object cannot be assigned a value later, so it needs an initializer at its definition.
questions.explanationCode:
const int x = 7;
// const int y; // error: no initializer

questions.text: What is the meaning of const int* p?

answers.text: p is an integer initialized to zero.
p points to an int that cannot be modified through p.
p is a constant pointer that cannot be reseated.
p points to memory that must have been allocated with new.

questions.explanation: In const int* p, the pointed-to int is const through this pointer. The pointer variable p itself can still be changed to point elsewhere.

questions.text: What is the meaning of int* const p = &x;?

answers.text: p is a constant pointer to an int.
p is a pointer to a const int.
p is a reference to an int.
p is a function returning int.

questions.explanation: The const applies to the pointer object p, so p cannot be reseated. The int it points at can still be modified through p.

questions.text: What does the const reference parameter below achieve for a large object?

answers.text: They force the argument to be allocated on the free store.
They disable overload resolution.
They make the function run at compile time.
They avoid copying while preventing modification through the parameter.

questions.explanation: const T& is a common way to pass large objects efficiently while preserving read-only intent.

questions.text: In the snippet below, what is required when square(4.0) is used in a constant expression?

answers.text: The function must return void.
The function must be a virtual destructor.
The call must be evaluable at compile time with constant-expression arguments.
The function must always allocate memory.

questions.explanation: A constexpr function can also be called at run time, but when a constant expression is required, the compiler must be able to evaluate the call.

questions.text: Which declaration prevents modification of a string object through the name greeting?

answers.text: std::string* greeting = "hello";
const std::string greeting = "hello";
std::string constless greeting = "hello";
mutable std::string greeting = "hello";

questions.explanation: A const std::string object cannot be modified through ordinary non-const operations.

questions.text: In the code below, what practical role does max1 demonstrate for constant expressions?

answers.text: They allow compile-time checks and values needed by compile-time constructs.
They postpone all arithmetic until link time.
They make every variable global.
They replace exception handling.

questions.explanation: Compile-time constants can be used in static_assert, array bounds, template arguments, and other places requiring constant expressions.

questions.text: What does the read-only interface below communicate and enforce?

answers.text: It hides every function from the linker.
It prevents code from using references.
It makes all objects have the same address.
It communicates and enforces which operations are read-only.

questions.explanation: const in function parameters, return types, and member functions helps callers rely on non-mutating behavior.

questions.text: In accept() below, what does the if statement choose between?

answers.text: Changing the return type of main.
Allocating a namespace on the free store.
Executing a statement when a condition is true and optionally another when it is false.
Including one header or another after compilation.

questions.explanation: The condition is contextually converted to bool. The selected branch determines which statement executes.

questions.text: What does the else part bind to in nested if statements without braces?

answers.text: The enclosing namespace.
The nearest unmatched if.
The first if in the function.
The next for loop.

questions.explanation: This rule can surprise readers. Braces make nested conditional logic clearer and less error-prone.

questions.text: When is the switch form below a natural choice?

answers.text: When selecting among several constant cases for one integral or enumeration expression.
When iterating until a pointer becomes null.
When declaring a class invariant.
When linking object files.

questions.explanation: switch works well for discrete alternatives such as command codes or enumerators. Each case label must be a constant expression.

questions.text: What happens in the switch below if execution reaches case 'y'?

answers.text: The compiler automatically inserts break.
The program always throws an exception.
The switch condition is recomputed.
Execution falls through into the next case unless another control transfer occurs.

questions.explanation: Fallthrough can be intentional, but it should be explicit and clear. An omitted break is a common bug.

questions.text: Why is the loop form below a good fit for copying these arrays?

answers.text: The loop needs exception handling around every element.
The loop should run at compile time only.
The iteration is naturally controlled by an index range.
The loop must declare a namespace before copying.

questions.explanation: A traditional for loop groups initialization, condition, and increment in one place, which is useful for counted iteration.

questions.text: Why is a while loop appropriate for the retry logic below?

answers.text: The condition can be checked only by static_assert.
The loop continues while a condition holds and can stop early after an answer.
The loop is declaring a class definition.
The loop must run exactly once for every namespace.

questions.explanation: while expresses condition-controlled repetition directly, such as reading values until input fails.

questions.text: What is the key difference between while and do-while?

answers.text: do-while tests the condition after the loop body, so the body runs at least once.
while can only use integer conditions.
do-while cannot contain statements.
while is evaluated by the linker.

questions.explanation: Use do-while only when one execution before the test is truly desired.

questions.text: What does break do inside a loop?

answers.text: It skips only the current expression and keeps the same iteration.
It declares a local variable.
It restarts main.
It exits the nearest enclosing loop or switch.

questions.explanation: break transfers control to the statement after the nearest loop or switch.

questions.text: What does continue do inside a loop?

answers.text: It prevents the loop condition from being tested.
It turns a while loop into a switch.
It skips the rest of the current iteration and proceeds with the next iteration step.
It exits the entire program successfully.

questions.explanation: continue is useful when the remaining loop body should be skipped for the current value.

questions.text: Why are braces recommended even for a one-line if or loop body?

answers.text: They convert local variables to globals.
They make the controlled block explicit and reduce maintenance mistakes.
They are required by the linker.
They make arithmetic faster.

questions.explanation: Adding a second statement later without braces can change program behavior. Braces prevent that class of bug.

questions.text: In the index loop below, what does the condition usually prevent?
questions.code:
for (int i = 0; i != v.size(); ++i)
    sum += v[i];

answers.text: It stops the loop before the first out-of-range index.
It makes every element const.
It sorts the vector.
It converts indexes to pointers.

questions.explanation: For a vector of size n, valid indexes run from 0 to n - 1. The test i < v.size() avoids using i == v.size().

questions.text: What does short-circuit evaluation mean for the && operator?

answers.text: Both operands are always evaluated twice.
The result is always an int.
The expression is evaluated by the preprocessor.
The right operand is not evaluated if the left operand is false.

questions.explanation: Short-circuiting is often used to guard a later operation, such as p != nullptr && *p == value.

questions.text: What is the main advantage of the range-for loop below over a manual index loop?
questions.code:
for (auto x : values)
    std::cout << x << '\n';

answers.text: It disables const correctness.
It always copies the entire container before looping.
It iterates directly over elements and avoids many indexing mistakes.
It can iterate only over built-in arrays of int.

questions.explanation: A range-for loop is concise and safe for simple traversal. The choice of value, reference, or const reference controls copying and mutation.

questions.text: In the range-for loop below, what does auto& mean for each iteration?
questions.code:
for (auto& x : values)
    ++x;

answers.text: x is a namespace alias.
x refers to the actual element, so modifications affect the container element.
x is a copy that cannot change the container.
x is always a pointer to the first element.

questions.explanation: Using a non-const reference in a range-for loop lets the loop body modify each element in place.

questions.text: Which declaration creates a pointer to an int?

answers.text: int* p;
int& p;
int p*;
pointer<int> p;

questions.explanation: int* is the built-in pointer type for objects of type int. The pointer can hold the address of an int object.
questions.explanationCode:
int value = 7;
int* p = &value;

questions.text: What does &x produce when x is an object?

answers.text: A copy of x converted to double.
The size of x in bytes.
The namespace containing x.
The address of x.

questions.explanation: The unary address-of operator returns a pointer to its operand, unless overloaded for a class type.

questions.text: In the code below, what does *p mean?

answers.text: The number of bytes in the pointer.
A declaration of namespace p.
The int object pointed to by p.
The address stored in p as a character.

questions.explanation: Dereferencing a valid pointer accesses the object at the stored address. Dereferencing an invalid or null pointer is erroneous.

questions.text: In the declarations below, what does nullptr express for pd and pi?
questions.code:
double* pd = nullptr;
int* pi = nullptr;

answers.text: delete
They intentionally point to no object.
void
std::cout

questions.explanation: nullptr is the null pointer literal. It is type-safe and preferable to using 0 or NULL in modern C++.
questions.explanationCode: // int x = nullptr; // error: nullptr is not an integer

questions.text: What is true about the built-in array below?
questions.code:
int values[4] = {1, 2, 3, 4};
int first = values[0];

answers.text: It contains four contiguous int elements indexed from 0 to 3.
It contains five elements indexed from 0 to 4.
It stores its length as a hidden run-time member function.
It can be assigned to another array with a = b.

questions.explanation: Built-in arrays have fixed size and contiguous storage. The programmer must stay within the valid index range.

questions.text: For the array below, why would values[4] be invalid?
questions.code:
int values[4] = {1, 2, 3, 4};
int first = values[0];

answers.text: Index 4 is always converted to index 0.
Built-in arrays require one-based indexing.
Only pointers can be indexed.
The last valid index is 3.

questions.explanation: C++ array indexing starts at zero. Accessing one past the last element is out of bounds and has undefined behavior.

questions.text: In the code below, what does p + 1 mean?

answers.text: The integer value stored at p plus one.
A request to allocate one more element.
A pointer to the next int element.
A pointer one byte later regardless of int size.

questions.explanation: Pointer arithmetic is scaled by the pointed-to type. For int*, p + 1 advances by sizeof(int) bytes.

questions.text: In the snippet below, what is the end pointer used for?

answers.text: It points to the first element of every array.
It marks the end position for iteration but must not be dereferenced.
It stores an extra valid element.
It resets the array length.

questions.explanation: Many C++ iteration patterns compare a current pointer with one-past-the-end. The end pointer is a sentinel, not an element.

questions.text: What happens to buffer in the function call below?

answers.text: It decays to a pointer to its first element.
It is copied with its full length automatically.
It becomes a std::vector.
It becomes a namespace.

questions.explanation: Array-to-pointer decay loses size information. Passing a size, using std::span, or using a container can preserve bounds information.

questions.text: Why is std::vector often safer than a built-in array for variable-size sequences?

answers.text: It disables indexing completely.
It stores every element as void*.
It can only hold bool values.
It manages storage and knows its size.

questions.explanation: std::vector grows dynamically, owns its elements, and exposes size(). Bounds still matter, but the size is available.

questions.text: What does delete[] require?

answers.text: A reference created by a range-for loop.
A namespace-qualified function name.
A pointer value returned by new[] for a compatible array allocation.
Any pointer into the middle of a local array.

questions.explanation: Mismatching allocation and deallocation forms is a serious error. Prefer standard containers and smart pointers to manual array ownership.

questions.text: What is a dangling pointer?

answers.text: A pointer declared inside a namespace.
A pointer that refers to an object whose lifetime has ended.
A pointer that is explicitly set to nullptr.
A pointer to the first element of an array.

questions.explanation: Using a dangling pointer can access storage that no longer contains the intended object. Avoid returning pointers to local variables.

questions.text: What does const int* begin, const int* end commonly represent?

answers.text: A half-open range of int elements, from begin up to but not including end.
Two unrelated integer constants.
A closed range where end must also be dereferenced.
A pair of output streams.

questions.explanation: Half-open ranges are common in C++ because an empty range has begin == end and loop termination is simple.

questions.text: Why is accessing a pointer after delete dangerous?

answers.text: delete automatically changes every copy of the pointer to nullptr.
delete converts the pointer to a reference.
delete makes the object global.
The pointer value may still exist, but the object it referred to no longer does.

questions.explanation: After deallocation, all outstanding raw pointers to that object are invalid. Owning resource wrappers help prevent this bug.

questions.text: What do Vector and Color demonstrate in the snippet below?

answers.text: Only a macro defined by the preprocessor.
Only a source file compiled separately.
A type introduced by a programmer, such as a struct, class, or enumeration.
Only a built-in arithmetic type.

questions.explanation: User-defined types let programs model domain concepts directly instead of using only primitive values.

questions.text: Why define a type such as Date instead of passing three unrelated ints everywhere?

answers.text: It forces every value to be stored as a string.
The type groups related data and lets operations enforce meaningful rules.
It prevents the program from using functions.
It makes all dates global variables.

questions.explanation: A named type communicates intent and can validate combinations such as month and day values.

questions.text: What is the purpose of an interface for a user-defined type?

answers.text: It defines how other code is meant to use the type.
It exposes every byte of representation as public data.
It disables construction.
It replaces all member functions with macros.

questions.explanation: A clear interface separates the operations clients rely on from representation choices that may change.

questions.text: In the Vector type below, what does representation mean?

answers.text: The command used to invoke the compiler.
The keyword used to include a header.
The return value of main.
The data members and layout used to store the type state.

questions.explanation: For a vector-like type, representation might include a pointer to elements and an integer size.

questions.text: Why is hiding representation often useful?

answers.text: It makes every operation slower by definition.
It requires all code to be written in namespace std.
It lets the implementation change without breaking code that uses the public interface.
It prevents objects from being initialized.

questions.explanation: Encapsulation is a way to protect invariants and reduce coupling between users of a type and its implementation.

questions.text: What is an invariant of a type?

answers.text: An overloaded output operator.
A condition that should hold for every valid object of that type.
A local variable that changes every loop iteration.
A compiler option for linking object files.

questions.explanation: For example, a vector-like type might require size >= 0 and elements != nullptr when size > 0.

questions.text: What role does the constructor below play in a user-defined type?

answers.text: It initializes a new object and can establish the type invariant.
It destroys an object at the end of its lifetime.
It declares a namespace alias.
It links object files.

questions.explanation: Constructors are called when objects are created. They are the right place to initialize members consistently.

questions.text: Why should operations on a type preserve the type invariant?

answers.text: The compiler cannot compile member functions otherwise.
It makes every operation constexpr.
It removes the need for destructors.
A valid object should remain valid after public operations complete successfully.

questions.explanation: If member functions leave an object in an invalid state, later operations can fail in unrelated places.

questions.text: What is the difference between a type and an object of that type?

answers.text: They are always the same thing in C++.
An object can never have a user-defined type.
The type is the set of rules and representation; an object is a concrete instance in storage.
A type exists only at run time and an object exists only at compile time.

questions.explanation: For example, Vector can be a type, while Vector v(10); creates one object of that type.

questions.text: Why are small, focused user-defined types often better than loose groups of variables?

answers.text: They require every member to be public.
They make invalid combinations harder to express and operations easier to find.
They eliminate all run-time costs in every build.
They forbid separate compilation.

questions.explanation: A good type collects data and behavior that belong together, which improves correctness and readability.

questions.text: What kind of C++ feature do Color and Traffic_light demonstrate below?

answers.text: An enumeration.
A raw pointer.
A preprocessor include.
A destructor.

questions.explanation: Enumerations give names to a fixed set of values such as traffic_light::red, traffic_light::yellow, and traffic_light::green.

questions.text: What does abstraction mean in C++ type design?

answers.text: Avoiding all user-defined types.
Replacing compile-time checks with comments.
Writing every function directly inside main.
Presenting essential operations while hiding unnecessary implementation details.

questions.explanation: Abstraction lets code use a concept such as vector, date, or complex number without depending on every internal detail.

questions.text: What does the struct definition below introduce?

answers.text: A linker script.
A built-in integer alias only.
A user-defined type with named members.
A preprocessor macro only.

questions.explanation: A struct can group multiple values into one object and can also have member functions, constructors, and other members.

questions.text: In the struct below, what is the default access level for sz and elem?

answers.text: implementation-defined
public
private
protected only

questions.explanation: struct and class are very similar. The key default difference is that struct members are public by default, while class members are private by default.

questions.text: Which expression accesses member x of a Point object named p?
questions.code:
struct Point { int x; int y; };
Point p {1, 2};

answers.text: p.x
p->x when p is not a pointer
Point::p.x
x.p

questions.explanation: The dot operator accesses a member through an object. The arrow operator is used through a pointer.

questions.text: Which expression accesses member x through the Point* named p below?

answers.text: p.x
Point.x
&p.x
p->x

questions.explanation: p->x is shorthand for (*p).x. It first dereferences the pointer and then accesses the member.

questions.text: What does the braced initialization below do for the simple struct?
questions.code:
struct Point { int x; int y; };
Point p {1, 2};

answers.text: It calls the linker for each member.
It sorts members alphabetically before initialization.
It initializes members in declaration order from a braced list.
It initializes only private members.

questions.explanation: For struct Point { int x; int y; };, Point p{1, 2}; initializes x to 1 and y to 2.

questions.text: Why must the struct definition below end with a semicolon?

answers.text: It turns all members private.
The semicolon terminates the declaration of the type.
It calls the default constructor.
It closes the namespace std.

questions.explanation: A missing semicolon after a class or struct definition is a syntax error.

questions.text: When is a struct with public data members a good fit?

answers.text: When the type is a simple data aggregate with no invariant to protect.
When every member update must validate complex rules.
When representation must remain hidden from all users.
When the type cannot be copied.

questions.explanation: Simple coordinate-like aggregates can be clear as structs. Types with invariants usually benefit from private representation and member functions.

questions.text: What does the Counter struct below demonstrate?

answers.text: No, a struct can contain only int members.
No, member functions are allowed only in namespace std.
Only if the struct is allocated with new.
Yes, a struct can have member functions just like a class.

questions.explanation: struct and class share the same member capabilities. The default access level is the main syntactic difference.

questions.text: What does passing Vector by const reference below avoid?
questions.code: double sum(const Vector& v);

answers.text: Calling the function.
Using the dot operator.
Copying the whole struct while still preventing modification through the parameter.
Declaring the struct type.

questions.explanation: const T& is useful for read-only access to larger objects without the cost of copying.

questions.text: What is the effect of assigning one simple struct object to another?

answers.text: The operation is forbidden for every struct.
Each assignable member is assigned from the corresponding member.
Only the first member is assigned.
The assignment always compares object addresses.

questions.explanation: The compiler can generate memberwise assignment for simple structs when all members support assignment.

questions.text: What does equality comparison require for a struct in pre-C++20 code?

answers.text: A suitable operator== must be provided if equality is needed.
Every struct automatically converts to bool.
The compiler compares member names as strings.
Only public members are compared by the linker.

questions.explanation: C++ does not universally infer all desired comparisons for every type in older standards. Define comparisons that match the type meaning.

questions.text: What is a nested struct?

answers.text: A struct whose first member is always a pointer.
A struct declared only in a build script.
A struct with no semicolon.
A struct declared inside another class or struct scope.

questions.explanation: Nested types can express that a helper type belongs to the enclosing type interface or implementation.

questions.text: Why can direct public access to the members below be risky for types with invariants?

answers.text: Public access disables the compiler.
Public access makes every member a function.
Any caller can put the object into an invalid state.
Public access prevents objects from occupying memory.

questions.explanation: If data must satisfy rules, member functions can validate updates and preserve the invariant.

questions.text: Which definition is a simple two-dimensional point aggregate?

answers.text: namespace Point { int x; int y; };
struct Point { int x; int y; };
Point struct { int x; int y; }
struct Point(int x, int y);

questions.explanation: The struct keyword introduces the type, the braces contain members, and the trailing semicolon completes the declaration.

questions.text: In the class below, what is the default access level for elem and sz?

answers.text: private
public
protected only
The same as the previous class in the file

questions.explanation: class members are private by default. Public members must be placed after a public: access label.

questions.text: In the Vector class below, what is the main purpose of the private data members?

answers.text: To make the members invisible to the compiler.
To force every object onto the free store.
To prevent construction.
To restrict direct access so the class can control its state through its interface.

questions.explanation: Private representation helps preserve invariants and allows implementation details to change safely.

questions.text: In the Vector class below, what does a public member function provide?

answers.text: A replacement for the linker.
A macro-expanded include guard.
An operation that client code may call on the object.
A variable that cannot be initialized.

questions.explanation: The public interface defines the intended operations of the class.

questions.text: What does the constructor initializer list below do?

answers.text: It disables all overloads.
It initializes data members before the constructor body executes.
It destroys members in reverse order.
It declares only local variables.

questions.explanation: Member initializer lists are the preferred way to initialize members, especially const members, references, and class-type members.

questions.text: Inside increment() below, what does this refer to?

answers.text: The object for which the member function was called.
The first global object in the program.
The namespace containing the class.
The compiler command line.

questions.explanation: this is an implicit pointer available inside non-static member functions. It lets code refer to the current object.

questions.text: What does const promise for the size() member function below?

answers.text: It can be called only during compilation.
It deletes the object after returning.
It can access only global variables.
It will not modify the observable state of the object through this call.

questions.explanation: A const member function can be called on const objects and treats this as a pointer to const.

questions.text: What is RAII in C++ class design?

answers.text: Replacing all classes with macros.
Allocating every object in namespace std.
Binding resource ownership to object lifetime so constructors acquire resources and destructors release them.
Reading all input with integers only.

questions.explanation: RAII makes cleanup automatic during normal execution and stack unwinding, which is essential for exception-safe code.

questions.text: When is a destructor called for an automatic object?

answers.text: Before the constructor body starts.
When the object lifetime ends, such as when control leaves its block.
Only when main returns 0.
Only when a pointer is dereferenced.

questions.explanation: Destructors release resources owned by the object. Automatic objects are destroyed automatically at scope exit.

questions.text: Why might the constructor below throw an exception?

answers.text: To report that a valid object cannot be constructed.
To skip member initialization after success.
To convert the object into a namespace.
To force clients to use public data members.

questions.explanation: If construction cannot establish the invariant, throwing prevents creation of an invalid object.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the explicit keyword on a single-argument constructor?

answers.text: It makes the constructor private.
It forces dynamic allocation.
It marks the constructor as a destructor.
It prevents unintended implicit conversions through that constructor.

questions.explanation: explicit is useful when a conversion would be surprising, such as converting an int to a complex object accidentally.

questions.text: What representation does the Vector class below use for dynamic elements?

answers.text: Only a single bool flag.
Only a string literal naming the type.
A handle containing a pointer to elements and a size.
Only a namespace with no objects.

questions.explanation: The object can store a pointer and a size while the elements live in separately allocated storage.

questions.text: Why should a class that owns raw dynamic memory define or disable copying carefully?

answers.text: The compiler refuses every class with a destructor.
The default copy may duplicate the pointer without duplicating ownership correctly.
Raw pointers cannot be data members.
Copying always calls main.

questions.explanation: Owning raw pointers need copy control to avoid double deletion, leaks, or shared ownership mistakes. Standard containers are usually safer.

questions.text: What is encapsulation protecting in the Vector class below?

answers.text: The integrity of the object state and freedom to change implementation details.
The ability to compile without declarations.
The requirement that every member is public.
The address of main.

questions.explanation: Client code should depend on the public interface, not on private representation details.

questions.text: Which kind of class member is being used publicly in the code below?

answers.text: All raw representation data.
Temporary variables used only inside one algorithm.
The linker command.
Operations that maintain the invariant and expose useful behavior.

questions.explanation: A class interface should be small and meaningful. It should not expose representation just for convenience.

questions.text: What are Color and Traffic_light useful for in the snippet below?

answers.text: Replacing every function declaration.
Changing compiler optimization settings.
Representing a fixed set of named values.
Representing an arbitrary-length dynamic array.

questions.explanation: Enumerations are useful for states, modes, colors, commands, and other discrete alternatives.

questions.text: What advantage of enum class is demonstrated by the invalid lines below?

answers.text: It disables switch statements.
Its enumerators are scoped and do not implicitly convert to int.
It can store values of any unrelated type at run time.
It automatically creates virtual functions.

questions.explanation: enum class improves type safety and avoids polluting the surrounding scope with enumerator names.

questions.text: Given the scoped enumeration below, how do you refer to its red enumerator?
questions.code: enum class Color { red, green };

answers.text: Color::red
red without qualification in every scope
Color.red
std::Color::red

questions.explanation: Scoped enumerators are accessed with the enum type name and :: qualification.
questions.explanationCode: Color c = Color::red;

questions.text: What is an underlying type of an enumeration?

answers.text: The namespace where the enum is declared.
The first function that switches on the enum.
The stream used to print it.
The integral type used to represent its values.

questions.explanation: An enum can have a specified underlying type, such as enum class Color : unsigned char.

questions.text: Which declaration specifies the underlying type of a scoped enumeration?

answers.text: class enum Mode(unsigned char) { read, write };
Mode enum : unsigned char = { read, write };
enum class Mode : unsigned char { read, write };
enum class Mode uses unsigned char { read, write };

questions.explanation: The colon after the enum name specifies the underlying integral type.
questions.explanationCode: enum class Byte : unsigned char { min = 0, max = 255 };

questions.text: What does the switch below show about enum class values?

answers.text: Only if the enum class is declared inside main.
Yes, switch can select among enumerators of an enumeration type.
No, switch only accepts double.
No, enum class values can only be used with pointers.

questions.explanation: A switch over an enumeration can make named alternatives explicit and lets compilers warn about unhandled values.

questions.text: What risk does the unscoped enum below create?
questions.code: enum Color { red, blue, green };

answers.text: Their names enter the surrounding scope and can collide with other names.
They cannot have more than one enumerator.
They always allocate memory dynamically.
They cannot be compared in any expression.

questions.explanation: Plain enum values such as red can conflict with other red declarations in the same scope. enum class avoids that by requiring qualification.

questions.text: Why is the assignment below invalid?
questions.code:
enum class Color { red, blue, green };

int n = Color::red;

answers.text: Conversion from source code to object code.
Conversion from bool to bool.
Conversion from a function to a namespace.
enum class deliberately avoids implicit conversion to int.

questions.explanation: Avoiding implicit integer conversion prevents accidental arithmetic and comparisons with unrelated integer values.

questions.text: How can you get the integral value of an enum class enumerator when needed?

answers.text: Dereference the enumerator with *.
Include <iostream> twice.
Use an explicit cast to the underlying integral type.
Call delete on the enumerator.

questions.explanation: Scoped enumerations require explicit conversion for integer use, making the conversion visible in the code.

questions.text: What should enumerator names express?

answers.text: Names of object files.
Meaningful alternatives in the problem domain.
Compiler optimization levels only.
Arbitrary memory addresses.

questions.explanation: Good enumerator names make states and choices readable, such as traffic_light::green instead of the integer 2.

questions.text: What is a good reason to define operator++ for Traffic_light below?

answers.text: To provide meaningful behavior such as incrementing to the next state.
To make the enumeration lose its type.
To force every value to be printed as a pointer.
To prevent switch from compiling.

questions.explanation: Operators can be defined when they make sense for the abstraction. They should preserve the meaning of the type.

questions.text: Why can enum class improve interfaces like the ones suggested by the types below?

answers.text: It makes every function a template.
It removes the need for a return type.
It prevents all overloads.
It prevents callers from passing arbitrary integers where a specific choice is expected.

questions.explanation: A parameter of type Mode is clearer and safer than an int whose allowed values are only documented informally.

questions.text: What is modularity in C++ program design?

answers.text: Avoiding functions and types.
Compiling without a linker.
Organizing code into coherent parts with clear interfaces.
Putting all code in main.

questions.explanation: Modularity reduces complexity by separating responsibilities and making dependencies explicit.

questions.text: What benefit does extracting the operation below into a function provide?

answers.text: Functions force every object to be global.
Functions name operations, isolate details, and make code reusable.
Functions prevent variables from having types.
Functions can only be called from constructors.

questions.explanation: A well-named function can replace duplicated code with one checked implementation.

questions.text: What makes a module boundary useful?

answers.text: A small interface that hides implementation details.
A public global variable for every internal detail.
A requirement that all code share one namespace.
A missing declaration for every exported function.

questions.explanation: Users of a component should rely on what it promises, not on temporary implementation choices.

questions.text: Why should dependencies between parts of a program be kept small?

answers.text: The linker refuses programs with more than one dependency.
Small dependencies disable type checking.
Dependencies are allowed only for built-in arrays.
Small dependencies make code easier to change, test, and compile.

questions.explanation: Tight coupling makes changes ripple through the program. Clear interfaces keep the impact of changes local.

questions.text: What role would the declarations below usually have in traditional C++ organization?

answers.text: The executable produced by the linker.
A replacement for every source file.
Declarations that other translation units need.
Only run-time input values.

questions.explanation: Headers usually contain declarations for functions, types, constants, and templates that clients must see.

questions.text: What role would the definitions below usually have in traditional C++ organization?

answers.text: Only namespace aliases with no code.
Definitions of functions and objects that make up an implementation.
Only declarations that must never be compiled.
Only command-line arguments.

questions.explanation: Source files are compiled into object files. They typically include headers needed to check consistency with declarations.

questions.text: Why is a stable interface valuable?

answers.text: Implementation can evolve while callers keep using the same operations.
It guarantees that bugs cannot exist.
It requires every function to be inline.
It prevents the use of namespaces.

questions.explanation: A stable interface reduces the number of places that must change when an implementation improves.

questions.text: What is a good sign that code should be separated into a helper function or type?

answers.text: The code contains any semicolon.
The code uses int once.
The code is inside namespace std.
The same idea is repeated or has a clear independent responsibility.

questions.explanation: Repeating a concept invites inconsistent fixes. Naming it once often clarifies the program.

questions.text: What should a component expose through an interface like the one below?

answers.text: The exact object-code addresses of functions.
Private invariants with no enforcing operations.
The operations and types needed to use it correctly.
Every local variable used by its implementation.

questions.explanation: Exposing too much creates coupling. Exposing too little makes the component hard to use correctly.

questions.text: Why do named abstractions matter in large C++ programs?

answers.text: They prevent separate compilation.
They let programmers reason about higher-level concepts instead of every low-level operation.
They remove the need for testing.
They make all types interchangeable.

questions.explanation: Good names and abstractions help maintainers understand intent and keep changes localized.

questions.text: What is a drawback of one very large source file?

answers.text: Unrelated code becomes harder to navigate, compile incrementally, and change independently.
C++ forbids files with more than one function.
The compiler cannot parse comments in large files.
Classes cannot be declared in large files.

questions.explanation: Splitting code along coherent boundaries improves maintainability and build behavior.

questions.text: How can an interface like the one below help make code type-safe?

answers.text: All values are converted to int.
All errors are ignored until program exit.
Every function returns void.
Misuse can often be rejected by the compiler through specific types.

questions.explanation: Specific types such as enum class, Date, or span can prevent accidental misuse compared with raw ints and pointers.

questions.text: What would user.cpp become after preprocessing in traditional C++ compilation?

answers.text: A single function call at run time.
A namespace imported from std.
A source file after preprocessing, including the headers it includes.
The final executable after linking.

questions.explanation: Each translation unit is compiled separately into object code before linking.

questions.text: What does separating Vector.h and Vector.cpp below allow?

answers.text: Every function can have multiple unrelated definitions.
Different translation units can be compiled independently and linked together.
C++ statements can be interpreted one at a time without compilation.
Definitions can disagree with declarations safely.

questions.explanation: Separate compilation supports larger programs by avoiding recompilation of every source file for every local implementation change.

questions.text: What information does Vector.h provide to another source file?

answers.text: Declarations needed to type-check uses of functions, types, and objects.
The run-time values of local variables.
The operating system process identifier.
The complete linker output.

questions.explanation: A source file includes a header so the compiler knows the interfaces it is using.

questions.text: What linker responsibility is illustrated by user.cpp depending on definitions from Vector.cpp?

answers.text: It checks the syntax of each for loop before compilation.
It expands every #include directive.
It chooses variable names for local objects.
It combines object files and resolves references between definitions and uses.

questions.explanation: If a function is declared and called but no matching definition is linked, the linker reports an unresolved reference.

questions.text: What is a typical cause of an unresolved external symbol linker error?

answers.text: A comment contains punctuation.
An int is initialized with zero.
A function is declared and used, but its definition is missing from the linked object files or libraries.
A local variable has a short name.

questions.explanation: Compilation can succeed because the declaration is visible, but linking fails when no matching definition is available.

questions.text: If the definition below is placed in a header and included by multiple source files, what can it cause?
questions.code: int counter = 0;

answers.text: A variable is initialized in a block.
The same non-inline entity is defined in more than one translation unit.
A function is called exactly once.
A header contains only declarations.

questions.explanation: The one definition rule requires exactly one definition for many entities with external linkage across the program.

questions.text: Why are include guards or #pragma once used in headers?

answers.text: To prevent repeated inclusion of the same header contents in one translation unit.
To force the linker to ignore the header.
To make every function virtual.
To allocate dynamic memory for declarations.

questions.explanation: Repeated inclusion can cause duplicate type definitions and other errors. Guards make headers safe to include through multiple paths.

questions.text: What role does the line below usually have in a header for a non-template function?
questions.code: double sqrt_sum(Vector& v);

answers.text: A separate non-inline definition copied into every source file.
The run-time stack frame of the function.
The object file produced by the compiler.
The function declaration.

questions.explanation: The declaration tells callers the interface. The definition can live in one source file to avoid multiple definitions.

questions.text: Why can changing a private implementation source file be cheaper than changing a widely included header?

answers.text: Headers are linked at run time only.
Private source files cannot contain bugs.
Fewer translation units usually need recompilation.
Source files are never compiled.

questions.explanation: Header changes can force all includers to rebuild. Implementation-only changes are more localized.

questions.text: What does the one definition rule protect against?

answers.text: Returning a nonzero status code.
Inconsistent or duplicate definitions of the same entity across a program.
Using more than one namespace.
Writing more than one if statement.

questions.explanation: C++ needs a consistent meaning for each entity. Violating the rule can cause linker errors or undefined behavior.

questions.text: Why should a header that declares make_values() include what it needs?
questions.code:
#include <vector>

std::vector<int> make_values();

answers.text: So it can be compiled correctly no matter which source file includes it first.
So every include becomes a namespace.
So the linker can skip object files.
So all declarations become local variables.

questions.explanation: A self-contained header is easier to use and less dependent on accidental include order.

questions.text: What is a forward declaration useful for?

answers.text: Creating an object of incomplete type by value.
Calling every member function without a declaration.
Replacing the linker.
Referring to a type by pointer or reference without needing its full definition immediately.

questions.explanation: Forward declarations can reduce header dependencies, but a full definition is needed when object size or members are required.

questions.text: Why are templates often defined in headers?

answers.text: Templates are executed by the preprocessor only.
Templates do not participate in type checking.
The compiler usually needs the template definition to instantiate it for specific types.
Templates cannot use declarations.

questions.explanation: Unlike ordinary functions, templates are patterns that must be visible when instantiations are generated.

questions.text: Which build step checks the syntax and types inside user.cpp below?

answers.text: Stream insertion.
Compilation.
Linking only.
Program execution only.

questions.explanation: The compiler analyzes each translation unit. The linker later resolves cross-translation-unit references.

questions.text: What problem does the namespace block below help solve?

answers.text: They group names and reduce collisions between independently written code.
They allocate arrays on the free store.
They replace all functions with macros.
They force every name to be global.

questions.explanation: Namespaces create named scopes. Code can use qualified names to say exactly which declaration is meant.

questions.text: How do you refer to cout in the standard namespace?

answers.text: cout.std
std->cout
namespace cout
std::cout

questions.explanation: The :: operator qualifies a name with its enclosing namespace or class scope.
questions.explanationCode: std::cout << "ready\n";

questions.text: What does namespace graphics { class Window {}; } do?

answers.text: It imports every standard library name.
It executes Window at program start.
It declares Window inside namespace graphics.
It creates a local variable named graphics.

questions.explanation: The qualified name of the class is graphics::Window.

questions.text: What does the using declaration below do?
questions.code:
using std::cout;

cout << "ready\n";

answers.text: It disables namespace qualification globally.
It introduces one selected name into the current scope.
It imports every name from std.
It defines a new output stream object.

questions.explanation: A using declaration is narrower than a using directive. It can improve readability without importing an entire namespace.

questions.text: Why should the directive below usually be avoided in headers?
questions.code:
using namespace std;

cout << "ready\n";

answers.text: It forces many standard library names into every file that includes the header.
It makes std::cout unavailable.
It prevents the header from having include guards.
It turns every int into a string.

questions.explanation: A header should not unexpectedly pollute client scopes. Prefer qualification or narrow using declarations in limited scopes.

questions.text: What does the namespace alias below provide?
questions.code:
namespace fs = std::filesystem;
fs::path config = "settings.cfg";

answers.text: A second definition of every object in the namespace.
A replacement for all include directives.
A run-time pointer to the namespace.
A shorter or more convenient name for an existing namespace.

questions.explanation: For example, namespace fs = std::filesystem; can make qualified names shorter while preserving explicit qualification.

questions.text: What does the repeated namespace block below demonstrate?

answers.text: Only if it was declared inside main.
Only after deleting all previous declarations.
Yes, later namespace blocks with the same name add declarations to that namespace.
No, a namespace can contain exactly one declaration.

questions.explanation: This lets related declarations be spread across multiple files while still belonging to the same namespace.

questions.text: What is the global namespace?

answers.text: A hidden local block inside main.
The outermost namespace scope.
A required class named global.
A library that stores every vector element.

questions.explanation: Names declared outside all named namespaces are in the global namespace. Excessive global names increase collision risk.

questions.text: In the return statement below, what does ::count mean?

answers.text: Look for name in the global namespace.
Look for name inside std only.
Dereference a pointer named name.
Call a constructor named name.

questions.explanation: A leading scope-resolution operator qualifies a name from the global namespace.

questions.text: Why is putting user code into namespace std wrong?

answers.text: std can contain only local variables.
std is not a namespace.
The linker always deletes std.
The standard namespace is reserved for the C++ standard library except for specific allowed specializations.

questions.explanation: Adding ordinary declarations to std gives undefined behavior and can collide with implementation or standard library names.

questions.text: What is the nested namespace below useful for?

answers.text: Disabling all qualification.
Replacing function parameters.
Organizing names into a hierarchy of related scopes.
Storing objects inside other objects automatically.

questions.explanation: Nested namespaces can express ownership such as app::ui::Window, where ui is a subarea of app.

questions.text: What does argument-dependent lookup help with?

answers.text: Skipping all overload resolution.
Finding functions in namespaces associated with argument types.
Allocating arguments on the free store.
Choosing the integer size of an enum.

questions.explanation: ADL is why unqualified calls such as operator overloads can find functions declared next to the relevant type.

questions.text: What is an error in a C++ program?

answers.text: A condition where the program cannot correctly continue the intended computation.
Any use of a namespace.
Only a missing semicolon.
Only a nonzero integer value.

questions.explanation: Errors include invalid input, failed resource acquisition, violated preconditions, logic bugs, and unexpected run-time states.

questions.text: Why should error handling be designed explicitly?

answers.text: The compiler requires every function to throw.
Explicit error handling disables constructors.
It makes all functions return bool.
Ignoring errors often lets invalid state spread to unrelated code.

questions.explanation: A clear error strategy helps keep failures near their cause and makes recovery or reporting consistent.

questions.text: What precondition is being checked before element access below?

answers.text: A namespace imported by the function.
A dynamic allocation performed by the linker.
A requirement that callers must satisfy before calling a function.
A guarantee made only after the function returns.

questions.explanation: For example, a function that indexes a vector may require the index to be within range.

questions.text: What is a postcondition?

answers.text: A compile command.
A condition that should be true after a function completes successfully.
The first statement in main.
A pointer with no object.

questions.explanation: Postconditions describe the result or state promised by an operation.

questions.text: When is throwing an exception appropriate in a function like operator[] below?

answers.text: When a function cannot perform its task and local code cannot handle the failure.
For every ordinary loop iteration.
To return the common successful value.
To replace all type checking.

questions.explanation: Exceptions separate normal return values from failure paths and can transfer control to a handler with enough context.

questions.text: When is returning an error code often reasonable?

answers.text: When constructors need to report impossible initialization.
When the caller must never inspect failure.
When the function has no possible errors but needs a namespace.
When failure is expected and local callers naturally check the result.

questions.explanation: Error codes can work well for common, local, recoverable outcomes when checking is disciplined.

questions.text: Why are assertions useful during development?

answers.text: They are the only way to define a class.
They catch linker errors at run time.
They document and check assumptions that should always hold if the program logic is correct.
They replace all input validation for users.

questions.explanation: Assertions are best for programmer errors and internal assumptions, not for normal external input failures.

questions.text: Why is validating constructor input below different from an internal assertion?

answers.text: Input validation is only for namespaces.
Bad user input is expected at run time and must be handled in normal program logic.
User input is checked by the linker.
Assertions always recover from invalid input.

questions.explanation: External input can be wrong even when the program is correct. The program should report or recover from such cases.

questions.text: What does fail fast mean in error handling?

answers.text: Detect invalid states early instead of continuing with corrupted assumptions.
Ignore every error until program exit.
Always use the fastest arithmetic type.
Skip constructors for speed.

questions.explanation: Early detection makes errors easier to diagnose and prevents damage from spreading.

questions.text: Why is resource cleanup important in failure paths?

answers.text: Cleanup turns every error into success.
Cleanup is needed only for int variables.
Cleanup happens only during preprocessing.
Errors should not leak resources or leave partially updated state behind.

questions.explanation: RAII helps because object destructors run automatically when control leaves a scope, including during exception unwinding.

questions.text: What makes an error message useful?

answers.text: It always prints a pointer value only.
It changes the type of the exception.
It tells what failed and includes enough context to diagnose or correct the problem.
It hides the failed operation completely.

questions.explanation: Good diagnostics help users and developers respond. They should be accurate and specific without exposing irrelevant internals.

questions.text: Why should a function not silently continue after detecting an unrecoverable invalid state?

answers.text: Silent continuation automatically fixes the state.
Continuing can produce misleading results or corrupt later operations.
C++ requires every function to call exit.
The compiler cannot compile if statements.

questions.explanation: A function should either handle the failure, report it, or stop the invalid computation in a controlled way.

questions.text: What does throw do in operator[] below?

answers.text: It raises an exception object and starts searching for a matching handler.
It exits only the innermost for loop.
It declares a new namespace.
It converts every local variable to const.

questions.explanation: throw transfers control out of the current flow. Matching catch handlers are considered as the stack unwinds.

questions.text: What does the try block below do?

answers.text: It disables all exceptions inside the block.
It runs only at compile time.
It creates a namespace called try.
It encloses code whose exceptions can be handled by following catch clauses.

questions.explanation: A try block is paired with one or more catch handlers that can respond to thrown exceptions.

questions.text: How is the catch handler below selected?

answers.text: By always choosing the last catch block.
By comparing exception message text only.
By matching the thrown exception type against catch parameter types.
By choosing the handler with the shortest source line.

questions.explanation: Handlers are tried in order. More specific handlers should appear before more general handlers.

questions.text: Why does the handler below catch by const reference?

answers.text: It only works for integer exceptions.
It avoids copying and preserves dynamic type information for polymorphic exceptions.
It modifies the exception before reading it.
It forces the program to ignore the exception.

questions.explanation: catch (const std::exception& e) is a common pattern for standard exceptions because it avoids slicing.

questions.text: What is stack unwinding?

answers.text: Destroying automatic objects while control exits scopes during exception propagation.
Sorting stack variables alphabetically.
Converting stack objects to heap objects.
Skipping all destructors after throw.

questions.explanation: Stack unwinding is why RAII cleanup works when exceptions leave a scope.

questions.text: What should a destructor generally avoid?

answers.text: Releasing resources it owns.
Being called at object lifetime end.
Using private data members.
Letting exceptions escape during stack unwinding.

questions.explanation: An exception escaping a destructor during unwinding can terminate the program. Destructors should normally complete cleanup without throwing.

questions.text: What does noexcept on a function communicate?

answers.text: The function returns no value.
The function is a namespace alias.
The function is specified not to let exceptions escape.
The function cannot be called.

questions.explanation: noexcept can affect optimization and overload choices. Violating it by letting an exception escape calls std::terminate.

questions.text: What is std::runtime_error used for in the throw expression below?
questions.code: throw std::runtime_error{"configuration is missing"};

answers.text: Defining a namespace.
Reporting a run-time failure with a diagnostic string.
Declaring a pointer to raw memory.
Replacing every Boolean condition.

questions.explanation: std::runtime_error derives from std::exception and stores a message retrievable with what().

questions.text: What does catch (...) mean?

answers.text: Catch any exception type.
Catch only std::runtime_error.
Catch no exceptions.
Catch only syntax errors from the compiler.

questions.explanation: A catch-all handler can be useful at a boundary, but it loses type information unless it rethrows or handles generically.

questions.text: What does throw; do in the catch handler below?

answers.text: It throws a default int value.
It resumes execution at the original throw expression.
It deletes the exception object silently.
It rethrows the currently handled exception.

questions.explanation: Rethrowing preserves the original exception object and can be used after logging or partial handling.

questions.text: Why are exceptions not ideal for ordinary loop control?

answers.text: They can only throw bool values.
They disable destructors.
They are meant for exceptional failure paths, not common local decisions.
They cannot cross function boundaries.

questions.explanation: Normal control flow is clearer with if, loops, return values, and algorithms. Exceptions should report failures that disrupt the normal path.

questions.text: What exception-safety concern is illustrated by expecting v to remain usable below?

answers.text: The number of comments in a function.
The state of objects and resources when an operation exits because of an exception.
The color of compiler diagnostics.
The alphabetical order of catch handlers.

questions.explanation: Exception-safe code avoids leaks and preserves invariants even when an operation fails.

questions.text: What is the basic exception safety guarantee?

answers.text: No resource leaks and objects remain in valid states after an exception.
The operation always completes successfully.
No exception can ever be thrown.
All values are restored bit-for-bit.

questions.explanation: The basic guarantee allows state changes, but the program remains usable and invariants hold.
