questions.text: Why can the constructor below use an exception to report failure?

answers.text: They call the destructor of the fully constructed object first.
They convert the constructor into a normal function.
They bypass member initialization.
They prevent an object from existing when it cannot be initialized into a valid state.

questions.explanation: If construction fails, already constructed subobjects are destroyed, and no invalid complete object is returned to the caller.

questions.text: What is a class invariant?

answers.text: The command used to compile a source file.
A rule that applies only to namespace names.
A condition that must hold for every valid object whenever public operations can observe it.
A variable that changes every time a loop runs.

questions.explanation: Invariants define what it means for an object to be valid. Constructors establish them and member functions preserve them.

questions.text: Who is responsible for establishing the invariant in the constructor below?

answers.text: The output stream.
The constructor.
The linker.
The caller after using the object several times.

questions.explanation: A constructor should either create a valid object or report failure, usually by throwing an exception.

questions.text: Who is responsible for preserving an invariant after construction?

answers.text: The member functions that modify the object.
Only unrelated free functions in other namespaces.
Only the preprocessor.
Only comments.

questions.explanation: Any operation that changes object state must leave the object valid when it completes successfully.

questions.text: Why do the private data members below help preserve invariants?

answers.text: They remove the need for constructors.
They make every object constexpr.
They prevent the object from having storage.
They prevent arbitrary external writes that could break the object rules.

questions.explanation: If representation is private, all changes can be routed through functions that enforce the invariant.

questions.text: What should the constructor below do when its argument cannot satisfy the invariant?

answers.text: Turn all data members public.
Return a bool from the constructor body.
Report failure instead of creating an invalid object.
Silently store invalid values and hope callers notice.

questions.explanation: Constructors do not return ordinary error values. Throwing an exception is a common way to refuse invalid construction.
questions.explanationCode:
// Refuse construction instead of creating an invalid Vector.
Vector v(-27);

questions.text: What precondition is operator[] checking below?

answers.text: A function that runs after the destructor.
A requirement callers must satisfy before calling that function.
A guarantee that every call succeeds regardless of input.
A private data member automatically initialized to zero.

questions.explanation: For example, a vector element access operation may require the index to be within range.

questions.text: What is a postcondition of a member function?

answers.text: A condition promised after the function completes successfully.
A namespace used before the function call.
A pointer to the object before construction.
An include guard.

questions.explanation: Postconditions state what successful execution guarantees, such as a size increasing by one after push_back.

questions.text: Why should invariants be simple enough to state clearly?

answers.text: Complex invariants are ignored by the compiler automatically.
A vague invariant is checked more often at run time.
Only unclear invariants can use constructors.
Clear invariants are easier to enforce, test, and reason about.

questions.explanation: If a programmer cannot state the rule, it is hard to know whether operations preserve it.

questions.text: What is wrong with exposing a non-const reference to private representation without control?

answers.text: Returning references is always a syntax error.
It prevents the object from being copied.
Callers may modify the representation and break the invariant.
References cannot bind to data members.

questions.explanation: Interfaces should avoid back doors that bypass validation. Return const views or controlled operations when possible.

questions.text: What is a valid invariant for a simple Vector handle with elem and sz members?

answers.text: Every element must be a namespace.
sz is nonnegative, and elem points to at least sz elements when sz is not zero.
sz is always negative.
elem must always point to the object itself.

questions.explanation: The exact invariant depends on the design, but size and storage must agree for indexing and destruction to be safe.

questions.text: Why is an invariant stronger than a one-time input check?

answers.text: It must remain true throughout the object lifetime, not just during construction.
It runs only in the preprocessor.
It applies only to int and double.
It is the same as a comment.

questions.explanation: Any operation that mutates the object must preserve the invariant, or the object becomes unreliable.

questions.text: When can the assert below help with invariants?

answers.text: When replacing all validation of external input.
When hiding a linker error.
When declaring a namespace alias.
When checking internal assumptions during development or debugging.

questions.explanation: assert can catch programmer mistakes early, but normal run-time errors still need proper handling.

questions.text: What should public member functions assume about the object at entry?

answers.text: Every private member is accessible to callers.
The linker will repair invalid state.
The object invariant already holds.
The object has not been constructed.

questions.explanation: Public operations rely on the object being valid before the call and must leave it valid afterward.

questions.text: How does RAII support invariants?

answers.text: It removes the need to initialize members.
It ties valid resource ownership to object lifetime.
It forces all resources to be global.
It prevents destructors from running.

questions.explanation: A resource-owning object can establish ownership in its constructor and release it in its destructor, keeping ownership rules local.

questions.text: In the line below, what does static_assert check?
questions.code: static_assert(4 <= sizeof(int), "integers are too small");

answers.text: A condition that must be true at compile time.
A condition that is checked only after the program exits.
A stream insertion result.
A run-time exception handler.

questions.explanation: static_assert rejects the program during compilation if its constant-expression condition is false.

questions.text: Which expression is suitable for static_assert?

answers.text: std::cin.get() == 4
new int{4}
std::cout << 4
sizeof(int) >= 4

questions.explanation: The condition must be a constant expression. sizeof(int) is available at compile time.
questions.explanationCode: static_assert(4 <= sizeof(int), "integers are too small");

questions.text: What would happen if the condition in the static_assert below were false?
questions.code: static_assert(4 <= sizeof(int), "integers are too small");

answers.text: The assertion is ignored by the linker.
The condition is converted to true.
Compilation fails with a diagnostic.
The program throws only after main starts.

questions.explanation: A static assertion is a compile-time guard. It prevents building code when required assumptions do not hold.

questions.text: Why is the static_assert below useful in template code?

answers.text: It makes every template argument a pointer.
It can reject unsupported template arguments with a clear compile-time diagnostic.
It disables template instantiation.
It converts templates into macros.

questions.explanation: Template errors can otherwise be hard to understand. A targeted static_assert can state the required property directly.

questions.text: How do the two checks below differ?
questions.code:
static_assert(4 <= sizeof(int), "integers are too small");

assert(ptr != nullptr);

answers.text: static_assert is checked at compile time; assert checks a run-time condition when enabled.
static_assert can read user input; assert cannot.
static_assert releases dynamic memory; assert allocates it.
They are identical in every way.

questions.explanation: Use static_assert for compile-time assumptions and assert for internal run-time assumptions during development.

questions.text: Which declaration checks that int is at least four bytes?

answers.text: assert_static(sizeof(int) >= 4);
static_assert(std::cin >> x);
constexpr assert int >= 4;
static_assert(sizeof(int) >= 4);

questions.explanation: sizeof(int) is a constant expression, so it can be used directly in static_assert.
questions.explanationCode: static_assert(4 <= sizeof(int), "integers are too small");

questions.text: What kind of message does the static_assert below provide if the check fails?
questions.code: static_assert(4 <= sizeof(int), "integers are too small");

answers.text: A run-time recovery callback.
A linker script.
A diagnostic string explaining the failed compile-time requirement.
A replacement value for the failed expression.

questions.explanation: A message such as "int must be at least 4 bytes" helps explain why compilation stopped.

questions.text: Why should static_assert conditions be meaningful rather than decorative?

answers.text: The compiler requires one assertion per function.
They should protect real assumptions that the code depends on.
Decorative assertions are optimized into faster code.
Meaningful assertions cannot compile.

questions.explanation: A compile-time check is valuable when violating the condition would make the code incorrect or unsupported.

questions.text: Why is the static_assert below invalid?

answers.text: No, the condition must be a constant expression.
Yes, it waits until the variable changes.
Yes, but only inside main.
Yes, because all variables are compile-time constants.

questions.explanation: A value read at run time is not available to the compiler as a constant expression.
questions.explanationCode:
constexpr double local_max = 160.0 / (60 * 60);
static_assert(local_max < 299792.458);

questions.text: What is a good use of the static_assert below with a standard type trait?

answers.text: Catching invalid user input after deployment.
Opening a file at compile time.
Printing every element of a vector.
Checking that a template argument satisfies a required property.

questions.explanation: For example, a template can require std::is_integral_v<T> and fail early for unsupported types.
